“But the oil math was always about inbound tankers more than outbound tankers. Without a steady stream of inbound VLCCs, oil production shut-in continues, eating away at the inherent deficit in the global oil balance. It doesn’t matter whether China is reducing imports or refinery runs; the molecule isn’t replaced.

And so far, the inflow is a trickle. No surprise, because the IRGC is firmly aware of the math. During the 60-day negotiation period, they will max out this leverage and throttle the flow under the disguise of granting permits. By throttling permits, they can effectively control who comes in and who goes out. So you would be a fool if you think they don’t know that controlling the inflow of tankers will ultimately decide how much production shut-in returns.

In addition to the logic I explained above, it is now evident, based on discussions with tanker owners, that the tanker rates offered for ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz are astronomical compared with other regions. Not only is the price insanely high, but operators are having trouble assembling crews willing to take the risk of going in.

So no, the issue is far from resolved, yet everyone still thinks it will somehow be resolved because both sides signed an MOU. Just wait until Trump finds out that IRGC is throttling flows and not actually de-mining the Strait of Hormuz. Will he just ignore this reality and play along or will he have to face the inevitable choice of escalation?

I don’t know, and I don’t really care. What I do care is the number of tankers going in, and right now, the market is going to be very wrong about this because the IRGC is going to throttle flows.”

EIA inventory report:

U.S. commercial crude inventories fell by 6 million barrels, while the Strategic Petroleum Reserve declined by a further 9 million barrels to a fresh multi-decade low. Combined, total U.S. crude stockpiles have fallen by around 100 million barrels since late April.
Inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the delivery hub for WTI futures, dropped to just 19 million barrels, the lowest level in 12 years and a threshold increasingly associated with operational constraints.
Gasoline and distillate inventories rose by 2 million and 3 million barrels, respectively, supported by robust refinery utilization and softer demand. Four-week average implied gasoline demand fell to the lowest seasonal level in 12 years, excluding the pandemic-affected period in 2020.
Total crude and refined product exports slowed to 12.1 million barrels per day, while U.S. crude production held steady at 13.8 million barrels per day.
Oil prices reset as stranded Gulf barrels head for market https://t.co/QG50uLoja3 via @saxobank

Trump is now turning his attention to refiners and fuel producers, criticizing the slow pace of declines in retail gasoline prices following the sharp drop in crude oil. However, the refining sector's response remains constrained by historically low inventories of refined products, particularly in the middle distillate complex.

As the charts highlight, US gasoline crack spreads remain elevated despite the recent easing in crude prices. The biggest beneficiary of the reopening of Middle East export flows has so far been the distillate market, with gasoil and diesel cracks falling from their March peaks. This reflects the fact that much of the additional crude now reaching the market is medium-to-heavy Gulf crude, which yields a higher proportion of middle distillates such as diesel, gasoil and jet fuel.

While increased crude availability should eventually help ease refining margins and lower fuel prices, depleted product inventories accumulated during the Strait of Hormuz disruption continue to support cracks. The combination of peak summer demand for gasoline keeping prices at the pumps seasonally elevated, refiners – for now - remain incentivized to maximize diesel and jet fuel production, delaying a more pronounced decline in gasoline prices.

#Commodities:

The Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index is heading for its worst monthly performance since 2022, currently down around 10% in June and reducing its year-to-date gain to 15%. All sectors are trading lower this month, led by a 16% slump in energy and a 12% decline in precious metals. Industrial metals and agriculture are both down around 6%, highlighting the broad-based nature of the latest commodity market correction, with the main drivers being the US-Iran peace deal, a hawkish Fed, and a stronger dollar.

Gold fell for a second session, pressured by a stronger dollar amid a technology-led equity selloff. Limited support came from rising Treasury bonds as lower energy prices ease concerns about inflation and reduce the need for additional Fed tightening. Instead, gold's unusually strong positive correlation with the S&P 500 continues to weigh on prices, pushing the metal into the key USD 4,000–4,100 support zone. A break below could trigger a fresh wave of capitulation and technical selling. Watch US equities, the dollar, and silver for direction, with the latter now challenging its March low near USD 61.

Brent crude slipped below USD 77 per barrel, leaving prices less than 10% above pre-war levels, as tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues to normalise. The International Maritime Organization said it has received safety assurances supporting the passage of hundreds of vessels out of the Persian Gulf. The market is increasingly pricing a steady return of stranded supply following one of the most severe disruptions in recent history. UAE exports have already recovered to around 85% of pre-war levels, adding to expectations of improving supply availability in the weeks ahead.

Cocoa retreated from a six-week high after Monday's 10% surge, as rising El Niño-related production concerns were offset by favourable near-term weather forecasts in West Africa. Improved rainfall across key growing regions has boosted crop development prospects, highlighting the ongoing tug-of-war between longer-term weather risks and near-term supply conditions.

📷DOW NASDAQ S&P 500 RUSSELL 2000

US stocks ended mixed as tech and semiconductor weakness persisted while falling oil and Fed stress test results supported cyclicals and financials ahead of key inflation and growth data

The Dow closed higher while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq extended their losing streak to a third session amid continued pressure on growth stocks tied to AI valuation concerns and earlier chip sector selling.

Tech and semiconductor names lagged, with Qualcomm (QCOM), Sandisk (SNDK) and the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) edging lower, while Cerebras (CBRS) fell after issuing a disappointing post-IPO margin outlook.

Financials advanced after major banks including JPMorgan Chase (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) passed the Federal Reserve’s annual stress test, announced share repurchases and dividend hikes, and were deemed able to withstand large losses.

Wendy’s (WEN) jumped following a Reddit-driven meme rally focused on its high short interest and the hiring of a new CFO, with heavy retail trading activity pushing the stock intraday to multi-month highs.
Homebuilders such as KB Home (KBH) gained after passage of an affordable housing bill, lifting the group on expectations of increased demand and supportive policy tailwinds.
After the close, Micron (MU) reported Q3 results that beat revenue and EPS expectations and issued strong AI-driven Q4 guidance, sending the shares significantly higher in after-hours trading following earlier intraday weakness.
Traders are looking ahead to Thursday’s PCE inflation report, a GDP revision and weekly jobless claims for further clarity on the Fed’s policy path, along with market follow-through from Micron (MU)’s earnings and other corporate results.

Wednesday was a tale of two tapes separated by a closing bell. During the session, the Nasdaq slipped -0.43% to 25,476.64, the S&P 500 eased -0.10% to 7,358.22, and the Dow gained +0.35% to 51,848.90. The Russell 2000 added +0.37%. The VIX fell -4.41% to 18.63. The 10-year Treasury yield dropped below 4.5% to 4.40%. The session opened strong, with the Nasdaq up more than half a percent at midday, before fading into the close as semiconductor volatility persisted. Distribution days climbed to 8 on the Nasdaq and 4 on the S&P 500. Exposure guidance was downgraded again to 60%-80%. Eight distribution days on the Nasdaq is the most elevated count of the entire uptrend, and the second exposure reduction this month reinforces that the tape is under genuine stress, even as the macro picture improves.

MarketSurge stock action was frozen with all dashes, but the snapshot told the story in one line: Nasdaq extends losses, but Micron jumps late. That last part matters more than everything else that happened during the regular session.

After the bell, $MU delivered. EPS of $25.11 crushed the $20.39 estimate. Revenue came in at $41.5 billion versus $35.1 billion expected. Q4 guidance of $49-51 billion obliterated the $43.2 billion consensus. Adjusted gross margins topped 84.9% against expectations of 81.83%. Micron also announced a strategic supply agreement with Anthropic for memory and storage, and declared a $0.15 quarterly dividend. The stock surged more than 13% after hours. This is the print the semiconductor complex needed. After the SOX dropped 7.9% on Tuesday and the entire AI infrastructure trade spent three weeks repricing on valuation and debt-funding concerns, $MU just answered the fundamental question: is the demand real? At $41.5 billion in revenue with guidance nearly 20% above the street, the answer is unambiguous. The question now is whether Thursday's open translates that beat into a sector-wide bid or whether the elevated distribution count and reduced exposure guidance keep the lid on.

Oil was the other major mover. Brent crude fell -4.33% to $73.74, its lowest level since before the U.S. and Israel first struck Iran at the end of February. WTI dropped -3.92% to $70.34, the lowest since early March. The entire Hormuz premium has now been unwound. Trump confirmed Wednesday that Iran has agreed to no tolls, insurance costs, or charges for ships transiting the strait. If these levels hold, the energy-driven inflation impulse that pushed headline CPI to 4.2% is functionally over. The next inflation print should begin reflecting this relief, and the Fed's case for a hike weakens with every dollar lower in crude.

Gold fell -3.20% to $4,016.82, dropping below the $4,000 level intraday as the dollar strengthened on rate-hike expectations. JPMorgan raised its year-end S&P 500 target from 7,600 to 7,800, citing earnings resilience despite the volatility. But the same report warned of strong potential for a reversal or flash crash given anticipated rate hikes and high levels of speculative trading.

$CBRS crashed after posting its first earnings report since its May IPO, with management forecasting a decline in core gross margins. $FDX shed roughly 6% after a narrow revenue beat. $KBH gained 3% on a homebuilder revenue surprise. News that South Korea's SK Hynix is planning a nearly $30 billion U.S. listing added supply concerns to the AI memory group, though $MU's after-hours beat may neutralize that overhang.

From here, the regular session was forgettable. The after-hours session changed everything. $MU's beat is the kind of print that can reset a sector narrative. Revenue 18% above consensus. Guidance 15-20% above the street. Margins expanding. An Anthropic supply deal adding another tier of AI demand validation. The semiconductor selloff that erased $1.4 trillion in market value this month just ran into the hardest wall of fundamental data the bears have seen. Oil at $70 WTI removes the inflation catalyst. $MU at 13%+ after hours removes the demand question. Whether the tape can turn those two inputs into a sustained recovery or whether the 8 distribution days and 60%-80% exposure guidance keep the pressure on is Thursday's question to answer.

The StrikeZone for Thursday, June 25:

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 25, 2026

Market Brief

The People’s Bank of China will conduct a 500-billion-yuan MLF operation, marking the second consecutive month of net expansion.

Incremental margin trading funds have entered the market, with A-share margin and short selling balances surpassing 3 trillion yuan for the first time.

Securities firms have halted new stock-related cross-border TRS transactions; existing positions may be rolled over or closed but not forcibly liquidated.

US President Trump said Iran has informed the US that no tolls, insurance fees, or other charges are being levied on vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

Precious metals turned bearish as gold and silver prices plunged, with spot gold falling below the $4,000-per-ounce mark.

International oil prices slid sharply, with US crude and Brent crude both down over 4%.

Micron Technology posted stronger-than-expected earnings, sending its shares up 15% in after-hours trading.

Qualcomm unveiled its Dragonfly roadmap, signaling a full-scale entry into the AI data center market.

Top Stories

US President Trump posted on social media that Iran has informed the US that vessels passing through the Strait of Hormuz are no longer subject to any tolls, insurance fees, or other charges. Trump warned that if the information is false, the US will immediately terminate negotiations. Regarding the temporary suspension of signing a bill aimed at reducing housing costs and increasing supply, Trump stated he would not sign it, asserting that he understands real estate better than anyone and that interest rate cuts are key.

The International Maritime Organization (IMO) released operational details for an evacuation plan in the Strait of Hormuz, stating that over 11,000 crew members stranded on vessels in the Gulf will be evacuated in phases under a coordinated mechanism. Iran will manage traffic and navigation coordination along the northern route, while Oman and the US will coordinate the southern route. Vessels may choose their route based on individual risk assessments.

Expectations of further Federal Reserve rate hikes have weighed heavily on precious metals markets. On June 24, spot gold fell below $4,000 per ounce for the first time since November last year, retreating approximately 30% from its January high near $5,600. Spot silver also slipped below $60 per ounce, halving from its historical peak. At least eight major international banks—including Citigroup, JPMorgan Chase, and Morgan Stanley—have lowered their gold price forecasts.

Micron Technology reported adjusted revenue of $41.46 billion for the third quarter of fiscal year 2026, a year-over-year increase of about 346%, exceeding analyst expectations of $35.69 billion. Adjusted earnings per share (EPS) came in at $25.11, compared to an expected $20.49. Adjusted operating profit reached $33.68 billion, surpassing the $27.86 billion forecast, with an adjusted gross margin of 84.9%, above the 81.9% expected. Micron forecasts fourth-quarter adjusted revenue of $49 billion to $51 billion, well above the $43.24 billion analyst consensus. Post-market, its shares surged 15%.

Qualcomm held an investor day in New York, announcing accelerated transformation into a full-scenario AI computing services provider. Qualcomm officially unveiled the Dragonfly family of products for the AI data center market, with the Dragonfly C1000 data center CPU scheduled for release in mid-2028. Qualcomm reached a multi-generation strategic partnership with Meta, and Microsoft Azure will deploy Qualcomm’s high-bandwidth computing (HBC) chips. Qualcomm expects its data center business to generate billions of dollars in revenue as early as fiscal year 2027. Qualcomm also acquired AI software firm Modular for $3.9 billion to strengthen its cross-device AI software capabilities.

China Macro & Policy

Premier Li Qiang attended the opening ceremony of the 2026 Summer Davos Forum in Dalian and delivered remarks. He also participated in a business roundtable. Li stated that innovation-driven development is the key to China’s long-term economic growth and stability. Innovation cooperation is an inevitable choice to resolve global growth challenges. China’s industrial competitiveness stems not from subsidies but from its comprehensive advantages.

China’s Foreign Minister Wang Yi spoke by phone with Pakistan’s Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Dar. Dar briefed Wang on the new phase of negotiations between Iran and the US. Wang stressed that the next steps should focus on three priorities: consolidating the ceasefire and halting hostilities, restoring normal navigation through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure global supply chain stability, and supporting Middle Eastern nations in improving bilateral relations and exploring a new regional security architecture.

Reports indicated that two Japanese nationals were detained by Chinese authorities while exporting rare earth-related products. Foreign Ministry spokesperson Guo Jiakun stated that the two Japanese citizens were lawfully detained by Chinese authorities for violating Chinese law. Japan should educate and remind its citizens and enterprises in China to abide by Chinese laws and regulations.

China’s Ministry of Commerce issued the “Measures for the Investigation of Supply Chain and Industrial Chain Security.” The Measures stipulate that if measures or actions by foreign governments, regions, organizations, individuals, or international bodies harm or potentially harm China’s supply chain and industrial chain security, the Ministry of Commerce may initiate an investigation and take corresponding measures based on findings.

The Ministry of Commerce further improved procedures for handling reports of violations related to export controls on strategic mineral dual-use items. Any organization or individual may report suspected violations to national export control authorities. For verified reports, the Ministry may provide rewards to whistleblowers under relevant regulations.

The People’s Bank of China will conduct a 500-billion-yuan MLF operation on June 25. With 300 billion yuan of MLF maturing in June, this implies a net expansion of 200 billion yuan, marking the second consecutive month of net expansion and an increase of 100 billion yuan compared to May.

China Equities Markets

A-share markets rose amid volatility. The SSE Index closed up 0.11% at 4,110.81, the Shenzhen Component Index rose 1.24%, the ChiNext Index gained 1.41%, and the Star 50 Index surged 3.82% to a record high. The Wind All China Index rose 0.75%, with total market turnover reaching 3.31 trillion yuan. Semiconductor supply chain stocks surged, with PCB and optical communication hardware stocks leading gains, while micro-cap stocks retreated. Lianxun Instruments’ shares exceeded 2,500 yuan, up over 3,000% since its IPO; Zhenbao Technology debuted on the STAR Market and soared over 1,200%.

Hong Kong markets edged higher. The Hang Seng Index closed up 0.33% at 23,412.18, the Hang Seng Tech Index rose 1.81%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index gained 0.07%. Semiconductor stocks led gains, followed by biopharmaceuticals; chemical and financial stocks underperformed. Northbound funds net bought 15.75 billion Hong Kong dollars, with Jian Tao Group and Jian Tao Laminates receiving net inflows of 2.816 billion and 2.063 billion Hong Kong dollars, respectively.

Exchange data showed that as of June 23, A-share margin and short selling balances totaled approximately 3.001 trillion yuan, marking the first time they surpassed 3 trillion yuan. This follows six consecutive days of growth, with a cumulative increase of 127.489 billion yuan since the previous week.

According to Caixin, stock-related cross-border TRS (total return swaps) have been halted. Securities firms are suspending new stock-related cross-border TRS transactions as per regulatory requirements; existing positions may be held until maturity or closed without forced liquidation. Industry estimates place the current outstanding cross-border stock TRS volume between 200 billion and 300 billion yuan.

China Industry News

Zhong Zhihong, Chief Engineer of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, said China must adhere to the principle of moderate advancement by accelerating new infrastructure development, strengthening the planning and construction of next-generation communication and computing networks, building a multi-tiered computing infrastructure system, and deploying new network facilities such as low-altitude information infrastructure and satellite internet to create an integrated space-air-ground information network.

Five ministries jointly launched pilot programs for independent 5G industrial private networks, supporting large and super-large enterprises in raw materials, equipment manufacturing, consumer goods, electronics, defense technology, and energy and transportation sectors to build independent private networks. They encourage pilot cities for “5G + Industrial Internet” integration to lead in independent private network deployment.

The 17th Summer Davos Forum released the “2026 Top Ten Emerging Technologies,” which include ubiquitous power internet of things, direct lithium extraction, passive radiative cooling materials, precision fermentation technology, exosome-based drug delivery systems, personalized mRNA cancer vaccines, quantum simulation for drug discovery, world models, and lattice-based cryptography.

Seven ministries launched a special campaign to rectify illegal recycling and dismantling of scrap vehicles, targeting five types of violations including illegal collection, unauthorized sale of scrap vehicle parts, and other unlawful activities. In 2025, China recycled 9.8 million scrap vehicles, 4.7 times the 2020 volume.

Two ministries issued guidelines to address chaos in formaldehyde remediation and testing, focusing on falsified data, manipulated results, and fraudulent reports by testing agencies and remediation firms. Increased supervision and inspections will be conducted, with severe violators facing revocation of their certification.

The State Administration of Radio and Television sought public opinion on a draft management method for micro-dramas. Under the draft, micro-dramas will be classified into three categories based on investment and genre, subject to filing, public notice, and licensing requirements. Unlicensed or unreviewed content will be prohibited from broadcast.

The China Academy of Information and Communications Technology reported that domestic smartphone shipments totaled 27.639 million units in May, up 16.5% year-over-year. From January to May, total shipments reached 114 million units, down 3.6% year-over-year.

Fintech

Hong Kong’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan said the Hong Kong government and the Securities and Futures Commission will submit a bill to the Legislative Council this year to establish a regulatory framework for institutions providing virtual asset trading, custody, advisory, and management services, aiming for comprehensive oversight of virtual asset activities—including stablecoins.

Corporate News & Updates

The South Korean government is in talks with Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix regarding their next phase of large-scale semiconductor production investments, with an announcement expected soon. Combined investment could reach 30 to 40 quadrillion won.

DouBao’s large model has launched a paid professional version. The standard subscription costs 68 yuan per month for continuous billing, offering five times the quota of the free version. The enhanced plan costs 200 yuan per month, with four times the standard quota. The premium plan costs 500 yuan per month, offering ten times the standard quota.

A US official revealed that during a joint test with US intelligence agencies, Anthropic’s Mythos model successfully identified multiple security vulnerabilities in highly classified, high-security US systems within hours. The US government has since instructed Anthropic to prohibit foreign nationals from using its latest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5.

Supply chain sources say Apple has finalized key specifications for its foldable iPhone, including display, casing, and mechanical components, and has entered mass production preparation. Apple will begin mass production in late July and plans to release the device in September as scheduled.

OpenAI and Broadcom jointly unveiled their first custom AI inference chip, Jalapeño, designed specifically for large language model inference tasks, aiming to reduce costs by approximately 50%. Deployment at gigawatt scale is planned by the end of 2026.

Unitree Technology has reduced the starting price of its bipedal humanoid robot, Unitree R1, to 29,900 yuan and is now offering spot sales. The robot was first launched in July 2025 with a starting price of 39,900 yuan.

Global Macro & Policy

US Treasury Secretary Bessent said negotiations with Iran have included discussions on pricing oil and gas exports in US dollars. Venezuela is also returning to the dollar system, and Russia may rejoin dollar-denominated settlement after the end of the Ukraine conflict. Bessent expects US GDP growth to return to 3% this year.

Iran’s Parliament Speaker Kalibaf stated that regional security must be guaranteed by regional nations themselves. Iran views the withdrawal of foreign military forces from the region as a strategic goal and has declared readiness to reach security agreements with all Islamic countries, especially Gulf states, reinforced by economic cooperation.

The Federal Reserve’s latest stress test showed that 32 major banks are well-capitalized to withstand severe economic recessions and continue lending. The tested banks could absorb over $700 billion in losses under hypothetical scenarios, with capital declining only 1.6%—still above regulatory minimums.

Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi announced a long-term economic growth blueprint, planning cumulative investments exceeding 370 quadrillion yen ($2.3 trillion) over 14 years, with a focus on AI and semiconductors. Investment in AI and semiconductors alone amounts to 101.6 quadrillion yen, nearly one-third of the total plan.

Bank of Japan Governor Ueda said the central bank sees risks that inflation may exceed its 2% target and reiterated the need to raise rates at the appropriate time. The timing and pace of adjustments will depend on the impact of the Iran conflict and other factors. The June policy meeting summary also signaled a possible further rate hike.

The European Central Bank reported that oil price surges triggered by Middle East conflicts are expected to reduce eurozone real GDP growth by 0.4 percentage points in the first year of the shock. If high oil prices persist longer than expected, the eurozone economy will face greater downward pressure.

The Bank of Korea further strengthened its hawkish stance, stating in its latest semiannual Financial Stability Report that, given inflation pressures, economic conditions, and financial stability risks, it considers it necessary to raise policy rates at an appropriate time.

The US housing market continues to struggle under high interest rates. The US Department of Commerce reported that new home sales in May, at an annualized rate of 580,000 units, fell 7.3% year-over-year, falling short of economist forecasts and hitting the lowest level of the year.

Australia’s May core CPI rose 3.6% year-over-year and 0.4% month-over-month, both exceeding market expectations, reinforcing expectations that the Reserve Bank of Australia will maintain a hawkish stance.

Global Equity Markets

US major indices closed mixed. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.35% to 51,848.90, the S&P 500 fell 0.1% to 7,358.22, and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 0.43% to 25,476.64. Chip stocks continued to retreat, with Seagate Technology down over 4%, Western Digital down nearly 4%, Qualcomm down over 3%, and Micron Technology down 0.37%. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index fell 0.7%, with Microsoft down over 2% and Tesla down over 1%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index fell 1.77%, and Alibaba dropped nearly 3%.

European major indices were mixed. The German DAX fell 0.62% to 24,740.36, the French CAC 40 rose 0.54% to 8,385.49, and the UK FTSE 100 gained 0.31% to 10,461.63. Progress in US-Iran talks eased oil prices, reducing inflationary pressures and supporting rate cut expectations, benefiting French and British indices. However, Germany’s economic slowdown, with June services PMI hitting a 43-month low and industrial contraction dragging down the DAX.

South Korea’s stock market swung sharply. The KOSPI rose 3.26% to 8,471.02 after initially surging over 4% before briefly turning negative. The Nikkei 225 closed down 0.88% at 69,174.97. India’s S&P BSE Sensex closed up 1.04% at 76,991.22.

MSCI released its 2026 market classification review results, maintaining South Korea’s emerging market status. MSCI stated that despite Korea’s reform measures, fundamental issues remain unresolved. Limited offshore currency convertibility of the won was cited as the key barrier to reclassification.

Amid market volatility, South Korea’s financial regulator postponed the launch of weekly individual stock options, originally scheduled for June 29. The Korea Exchange had planned to introduce weekly options on SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Hyundai Motor, and LG Energy Solution to meet growing demand for shorter-term derivatives.

SK Hynix filed for a US IPO, planning to raise up to 45.45 trillion won ($29.4 billion) through American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) to fund a new chip factory in Korea, with an expected listing on Nasdaq on July 10. If completed, this would be the largest ADR offering in history.

Samsung Electronics is preparing a near-90-trillion-won ($58.6 billion) share buyback program to fund employee bonuses. The company previously agreed with its union to distribute performance bonuses in stock. Calculations indicate Samsung must issue approximately 93 trillion won in stock bonuses after tax.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market performed strongly overall, with most key interest rates declining. Policy bank bonds outperformed government bonds. The 30-year treasury futures contract closed down 0.12%. The People’s Bank of China conducted 662.5 billion yuan in reverse repurchase operations, net injecting 242.2 billion yuan. As the quarter-end approached, liquidity remained tight, but the D-R001 weighted rate fell over 4 basis points to 1.41%, while D-R007 rose nearly 5 basis points to above 1.54% due to seasonal funding needs.

Venezuela is set to launch its largest sovereign debt restructuring effort to date. Sources said Venezuela will publicly disclose its debt data in coming weeks, with total liabilities estimated at $240 billion—far exceeding market expectations of $150–200 billion.

US Treasury yields fell across the curve. The 2-year yield dropped 4.42 basis points to 4.146%, the 3-year yield fell 6.50 basis points to 4.144%, the 5-year yield declined 8.34 basis points to 4.178%, the 10-year yield dropped 10.07 basis points to 4.392%, and the 30-year yield fell 10.28 basis points to 4.841%.

Commodities

US crude futures fell 4.56% to $69.87 per barrel; Brent crude dropped 4.45% to $73.38 per barrel. Resumption of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz increased crude supply, eliminating geopolitical risk premiums. Market expectations of increased supply, combined with rising Fed rate hike expectations strengthening the dollar, pressured oil prices. JPMorgan Chase lowered its Brent crude price forecast.

International precious metals futures declined broadly. COMEX gold futures fell 3.21% to $4,016.40 per ounce; COMEX silver futures plunged 7.39% to $57.48 per ounce. Hawkish rate hike expectations strengthened the dollar, while reduced geopolitical uncertainty weakened gold’s safe-haven appeal. Weaker industrial demand and rising inventories pressured silver.

London base metals closed lower across the board. LME lead fell 1.06% to $1,913.50 per ton, nickel dropped 2.20% to $16,795.00, zinc declined 2.41% to $3,408.00, copper fell 2.58% to $13,026.50, tin dropped 3.23% to $49,500.00, and aluminum slid 3.63% to $3,115.00.

Oil prices have fallen 18% since early March, and US gasoline prices have followed suit. However, US President Trump remains dissatisfied. He posted on social media that major oil companies have not significantly lowered gasoline prices despite steep drops in crude costs. He has instructed the Justice Department to launch an immediate investigation.

FX & Currency Markets

On Wednesday, the onshore renminbi against the US dollar closed at 6.8052, down 187 basis points from the previous trading day. The offshore rate closed at 6.8092. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8195, a 24-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York close, the US Dollar Index rose 0.20% to 101.57. Non-US currencies were mixed: the euro fell 0.20% to 1.1359, the pound fell 0.26% to 1.3169, the Australian dollar fell 0.23% to 0.6900, the dollar-yen rose 0.14% to 161.8075, the dollar-canadian dollar rose 0.16% to 1.4233, the dollar-swiss franc rose 0.34% to 0.8124, and the offshore renminbi fell 184 basis points to 6.8132.

Japan’s growth strategy draft indicates the government is reconsidering the management of its $1.3 trillion foreign exchange reserves to enhance asset returns and supplement fiscal resources. The move has sparked market concerns over potential large-scale sales of US Treasuries.

This chart is showing the U.S. energy cushion getting stripped down. It combines crude oil, the SPR, gasoline, distillate, and jet fuel, so it is not just a crude inventory chart. It is a picture of the whole liquid fuels buffer. The dark green 2026 line has fallen almost vertically into week 25 and is sitting far below the 5 year pattern. That tells you the system has been drawing heavily on stored energy to absorb the Hormuz shock, refinery demand, exports, and disrupted global flows. The latest EIA data still show refineries running hard at 96.1% utilization, with crude inputs around 17.1 million barrels per day, while total commercial petroleum inventories fell slightly even as gasoline and distillate inventories rose. That mix matters because it says the upstream barrel cushion is tight, but end product demand is not uniformly strong.  

The nuance is that this can coexist with oil prices falling. People think low inventories should automatically mean higher oil, but price is about the next marginal condition. Brent is falling because the market is removing the Hormuz war premium as tanker flows improve and stranded crude starts moving again. Brent settled around $73.74, its lowest since before the Iran war, as supply disruption fears eased. But the inventory chart says the physical buffer is still thin. That means lower oil is not necessarily a clean all clear signal. It may be saying the market is now more worried about demand destruction than immediate supply loss.

This is a late cycle energy tape. The U.S. burned through a lot of cushion to stabilize the shock, but now the price is falling because the economy may not be able to absorb expensive energy anymore. That is not the same as abundance. It is fragility with weaker demand layered on top. If demand improves and inventories rebuild, this becomes a successful shock absorption story. But if oil keeps falling while storage remains depleted, the dollar stays strong, products build, and industrial indicators soften, then the message is darker. It means the market is not pricing comfort. It is pricing a slowdown after the emergency barrels have already been used.

Oil Falls to Postwar Lows
by Bloomberg  C. Charleston, A. Longley, G. Levin  | Wednesday, June 24, 2026
Oil's wartime price gains have evaporated as the US and Iran log progress in peace talks and more tankers openly transit the Strait of Hormuz, easing fears of a supply crunch.

West Texas Intermediate fell to settle near $70 a barrel, the lowest since the end of February just before the US and Israel launched their war on Iran. Brent, the global benchmark, settled below $74 a barrel, also the lowest since the war broke out.

"Oil is dropping sharply as the Strait of Hormuz remains open and traders start to focus on OPEC, including Iran, potentially moving to maximum production," Infrastructure Capital Management chief executive Jay Hatfield said. "We forecast that WTI could drop below $60 over the next two months as production ramps and stocks rebuild."

Vessels are transiting Hormuz with their satellite signals switched on, indicating growing confidence among shipowners about safe passage of the chokepoint, through which about a fifth of global oil supplies transited before the war. The International Maritime Organization also said it had received safety guarantees allowing hundreds of ships to exit the Persian Gulf.

Washington and Tehran have both flagged early progress in talks to end the war, although negotiations are likely to be protracted and claims from the two sides have diverged. In a sign of how much oil has been leaving Hormuz in recent weeks, the International Energy Agency estimates that the United Arab Emirates is exporting oil at nearly 85% of pre-war levels.

The resumed flows out of the Persian Gulf come as US data showed domestic crude inventories plunged to the lowest since 1984. Stockpiles at Cushing, Oklahoma, dropped below 19 million barrels, according to the Energy Information Administration, slipping below levels many traders view as operational minimums. Still, prices were little changed after the release, with traders focusing on broader signs of easing supply tightness.

A closely watched oil market indicator flipped into a bearish structure on Wednesday for the first time since February, with Brent's prompt time-spread trading in a shallow contango, with the nearest contract's price below the next month's. That structure typically signals expectations of oversupply.

"I think the physical markets are much weaker than most people anticipated in Asia and Europe with Brent now going contango as well," TP ICAP analyst Scott Shelton said. "This subtracts buyers from the oil market which makes it fall much more easily."

There's also been a collapse in prices for real-world barrels, with premiums for barrels from the North Sea to West Africa tumbling.

Oil prices are down about 40% from their high during the peak of the conflict, with the steady increase in traffic adding to workarounds that were put in place to mitigate disruption through Hormuz at the height of the war. The UAE alone has sold around 60 million barrels from inside the Persian Gulf in recent weeks.

The Republican-led Senate voted Tuesday to end the US war with Iran in a rare symbolic rebuke of US President Donald Trump. While the resolution is unlikely to force any changes in the administration's strategy, it represents the latest sign that the president lacks domestic support for the effort.

Separately, Trump said in a social media post he had ordered the Department of Justice to look into why gasoline prices haven't fallen faster as oil drops. The national average retail price has declined 14% since late May and is now below $4 a gallon, although it remains above the five-year seasonal average, according to data from the American Automobile Association. US retail diesel prices fell below $5 a gallon for the first time since mid-March.

Oil Falls to Postwar Lows Despite historically low US crude inventories, traders focused on improving Middle East supply flows and growing signs of potential oversupply.

A rumor caught my attention today in Baghdad about Iraq and OPEC 🇮🇶

I don’t know if it will ever happen, and honestly that’s not what interested me. What interested me was the question it made me ask.

What would it mean if Iraq eventually stopped thinking like an OPEC member and started thinking like one of the world’s next major investment destinations?

Most people would immediately jump to oil.

I wouldn’t.

Oil is only the beginning of the story. Capital is the bigger story.

Today, countries don’t compete only on natural resources. They compete for investment, talent, technology, manufacturing, data centers, global headquarters, financial institutions, and long-term strategic partnerships.

That shift changes everything.

If Iraq’s long-term strategy evolved from maximizing production quotas to maximizing foreign investment, it wouldn’t create opportunities only for energy companies.

It would change the conversation for banks, infrastructure developers, AI companies, cybersecurity firms, logistics providers, engineering companies, healthcare organizations, universities, private equity, venture capital, and every business looking for its next frontier market.

I’ve spent years working between the United States and the Middle East, and one lesson keeps repeating itself.

The biggest opportunities almost never announce themselves with certainty.

They usually arrive disguised as uncomfortable questions, political headlines, or ideas that most people dismiss too early.

The executives who build the next decade aren’t always the ones who react fastest.

They’re the ones who recognize a structural shift before everyone else does.

Whether Iraq ever leaves OPEC isn’t really the point.

The more interesting question is this:

What would Iraq have to become for the world’s largest companies to start looking at it differently?

That’s a conversation worth having.
